page.title: Demon King of the Sixth Heaven

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ๅคฉ้ญ]][[simple:Demon King of the Sixth Heaven]][[zh:็ฌฌๅญๅคฉ้ญ็]]



 


{{Nihongo|{{ill|Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|ja|ๅคฉ้ญ|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}}|็ฌฌๅญๅคฉ้ญ็|dairokuten-maล|qq=}} is a Japanese Buddhist deity. {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} identified himself as this deity
{{Redirect|Dairokuten-maou|The Sixth Heaven Demon King|Taka-jizai-ten|Shrine|Dairokuten Shrine|qq=}}
{{Refimprove|date=2021-09-22|qq=}}
[[File:Hokusai Dairokuten-maou.jpg|right|thumb|280px|The Sixth Heaven Demon King attacking Shakyamuni Tathฤgata and his disciples in [[:en:Katsushika Hokusai|Katsushika Hokusai]]'s ''Shaka Gonen-ezu-e''.]]
'''Tenma''' (ๅคฉ้ญ) refers to the Sixth Heaven Demon King, Ha-jun (ๆณขๆฌ, {{lang-sa|เคชเคพเคชเฅเคฏเคธเฅ|qq=}}, {{unicode|pฤpฤซyas|qq=}}, meaning "most wicked one"), a demon who hinders the practice of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. Also known as '''Tenshi-ma''' (ๅคฉๅญ้ญ), '''{{ill|Takijizaiten|lt=Takijizaiten|lt=Taka-jizai-ten|ja|ไปๅ่ชๅจๅคฉ|1=Takijizaiten|2=ja|3=Takijizaiten|4=zh|5=ไปๅ่ชๅจๅคฉ|6=th|7=เธเธฃเธเธดเธกเธกเธดเธเธงเธชเธงเธฑเธเธเธต|8=ru|9=ะะฐัะธะฝะธะผะธััะฐ-ะฒะฐัะฐะฒะฐััะธะฝ|10=แแแแญแแนแแญแแแแแแนแแฎแแฏแถ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-jizai-ten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''', or simply '''the Demon King'''. The term may also refer to spirits under the demonโs command (see {{ill|Mayen|ja|้ญ็ธ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mayen|1=Mayen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673902}}).<ref>[https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/P/6 pฤpฤซyas | Dictionary of Buddhism | Nichiren Buddhism Library]</ref><ref>[https://tibetanbuddhistencyclopedia.com/en/index.php/Papiyas Papiyas - Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia]</ref><ref>[https://www.wisdomlib.org/definition/papiyas Papiyas, Pฤpฤซyas: 7 definitions]</ref>
== Overview ==
The "Sixth Heaven" is the highest realm of the {{ill|six heavens of the desire realm|lt=six heavens of the desire realm|lt=Six Heavens of Desire|ja|ๅญๆฌฒๅคฉ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Heavens of Desire|qq=}} in the {{ill|Desire realm|en|Desire realm|qid=Q4447630|lt=Desire Realm|1=Desire realm}} ({{ill|Desire realm|en|Desire realm|qid=Q4447630|lt=Yokukai|1=Desire realm}}). According to the [[:en:Dร _zhรฌdรน_lรนn|Dร  zhรฌdรน lรนn]] (ๅทป9), "This heaven steals the fruits of others' cultivation for its own enjoyment, thus it is called 'Taka-jizai-ten' (heaven of free enjoyment of others' creations)." The ''{{ill|Qi Shih Jing|ja|่ตทไธ็ต|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qi Shih Jing|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (ๅทป1) describes it as "Situated above the Heaven of Free Enjoyment and below the Heaven of Brahma, where the palaces of Mara Pฤpฤซyas and other heavenly beings are located."
The ''{{ill|Past and Present Cause and Effect Sutra|ja|้ๅป็พๅจๅ ๆ็ต|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Past and Present Cause and Effect Sutra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' (ๅทป3) mentions the "Sixth Heaven Demon King," who is also referred to as the "King of Free Enjoyment." Drawing from these texts, the ''{{ill|Fo Zu Tong Ji|ja|ไป็ฅ็ตฑ็ด|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fo Zu Tong Ji|1=Chronicle of the Buddhas and Patriarchs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3755470}}'' (ๅทป2) states, "The scriptures explain that the Mara Pฤpฤซyas resides at the peak of the six heavens with a separate palace. Thus, Taka-jizai-ten equates to Tenma."
In the ''{{ill|Nirvana Sutra|ja|ๆถๆง็ต|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nirvana Sutra|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ๅคง่ฌๆถๆง็ต|1=Mahฤyฤna Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q375539}},'' Tenma is said to disrupt Shakyamuni Tathฤgata's teachings by impersonating monks, lay followers, saints, and arhats, preaching contradictory doctrines.
== Ha-jun in the ''Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra'' ==
The ''{{ill|Mahฤyฤna Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra|en|Mahฤyฤna Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra|qid=Q375539|lt=Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra|1=Mahฤyฤna Mahฤparinirvฤแนa Sลซtra}}'' begins with Shakyamuni Tathฤgata about to enter {{ill|Nirvana|en|Nirvana|qid=Q42927|lt=nirvana|1=Nirvana}}. Many, including the Demon King Ha-jun, arrive to pay homage. The sutra describes the following scene:
{{quote|
Ha-jun, using the Buddhaโs divine power, opened the gates of hell and provided pure water to alleviate the suffering of the damned, causing them to abandon their weapons. Teaching the Buddha's truths, he encouraged evildoers to abandon their ways. Ha-jun approached the Buddha, venerated his feet, and vowed to protect the {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} teachings and its followers. He offered mantras to those practicing the {{ill|Dharma|ja|ๆญฃๆณ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dharma|1=Shลpล|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11123494}}, ensuring their protection and comparing their mindfulness to the protective nature of a turtle withdrawing into its shell. As a final gesture, he sought the Buddha's acceptance of his sincerity. The Buddha declined Ha-jun's material offerings but accepted his mantra as a means to bring peace to all beings. Despite this, Ha-jun remained sorrowful, seated in a corner.|qq=}}
== The ''Lotus Sutra'' and the Sixth Heaven Demon King ==
{{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=ๆฅ่ฎ|4=ko|5=๋์น๋ |6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478ja|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=ๆฅ่ฎ|4=ko|5=๋์น๋ |6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q31147812=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} described the Sixth Heaven Demon King as an entity that hinders Buddhist practitioners from following the teachings of the ''{{ill|Lotus Sutra|en|Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}''. However, Nichiren also taught that the Demon King ultimately becomes an ally of strong believers of the ''Lotus Sutra.'' In Nichiren's writings, the Demon King is depicted on the ''Lotus Mandala'' to signify this transformation.
The Demon Kingโs challenges to practitioners serve to deepen their faith and provide opportunities to overcome their {{ill|Karma|en|Karma|qid=Q132196|lt=karma|1=Karma}} from past lives. By persevering, practitioners can reduce and extinguish karmic burdens, leading to greater spiritual merit. Nichiren emphasized that the hardships caused by the Demon King in the present life serve to mitigate suffering in future existences, embodying the principle of "turning heavy karma into light."

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Jissล-ji (Fuji)|ja|ๅฎ็ธๅฏบ (ๅฏๅฃซๅธ)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jissล-ji (Fuji)|1=Jissล-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452819}}
* {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|qid=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen|1=Suzuka Gozen}}
* {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|qid=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshikatsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}

* {{ill|Gozu Tennล|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennล|qq=}}
